2026-06-19T08:56:26.2311007Z [36;1mset -euo pipefail[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2311425Z [36;1m# actions/download-artifact (>=v5) flattens contents directly into `path/`[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2311947Z [36;1m# whenever exactly one artifact matches the pattern, ignoring[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2312452Z [36;1m# `merge-multiple: false`. Re-shape into the per-group subdir layout so[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2312983Z [36;1m# downstream validation sees a stable structure regardless of matrix size.[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2313406Z [36;1m# Upstream reference:[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2313866Z [36;1m# https://github.com/actions/download-artifact/blob/v8.0.1/src/download-artifact.ts[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2314732Z [36;1m# (see the `isSingleArtifactDownload || mergeMultiple || artifacts.length === 1`[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2315304Z [36;1m# branch). Remove this step once download-artifact stops flattening or[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2315753Z [36;1m# exposes an opt-out.[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2316130Z [36;1mexpected_count=$(echo "$EXPECTED_MATRIX" | jq '.group // [] | length')[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2316555Z [36;1mif [ "$expected_count" -eq 1 ]; then[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2316928Z [36;1m  group_id=$(echo "$EXPECTED_MATRIX" | jq -r '.group[0].id')[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2317422Z [36;1m  # Defence-in-depth: group_id is interpolated into a shell path. apm-prep[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2317974Z [36;1m  # produces a sanitised id today, but enforce a strict allowlist here so[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2318468Z [36;1m  # any future schema drift cannot smuggle traversal sequences.[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2318926Z [36;1m  if ! printf '%s' "$group_id" | grep -Eq '^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$'; then[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2319397Z [36;1m    echo "::error::unsafe group_id '$group_id' (must match ^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$)"[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2319784Z [36;1m    exit 1[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2320013Z [36;1m  fi[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2320356Z [36;1m  group_dir="/tmp/gh-aw/apm-bundles/${ARTIFACT_PREFIX}apm-${group_id}"[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2320746Z [36;1m  if [ ! -d "$group_dir" ]; then[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2321215Z [36;1m    mkdir -p "$group_dir"[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2321707Z [36;1m    find /tmp/gh-aw/apm-bundles -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -path "$group_dir" -exec mv {} "$group_dir/" \;[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:26.2322184Z [36;1m  fi[0m

2026-06-19T08:56:44.1890230Z [36;1m# Mask immediately to prevent timing vulnerabilities[0m

2026-06-19T08:56:44.1890998Z [36;1mecho "::add-mask::${API_KEY}"[0m
2026-06-19T08:56:44.1891287Z [36;1m[0m

2026-06-19T09:04:12.1717379Z       "rationale": "The default scaffold generated by apm hooks init produces a post-install command hook of `echo 'Installed:' && cat`. This is functionally correct (it shows the JSON payload on stdout) but strategically flat: a developer running apm hooks init sees nothing that makes them want to keep the hook. npm post-install hooks won mindshare because the first example was always 'run your build' or 'print a success message with color'. JSON does not support comments; the _comment/_type trick or a real second hook entry with a placeholder URL both work."

2026-06-19T09:04:42.0497648Z       "lines": "103-108",
2026-06-19T09:04:45.3738952Z       "description": "The discovery locations table documents only the Unix/Linux policy path (`/etc/apm/policy.d/*.json`). The implementation in `_get_policy_hooks_dir()` returns `C:\\ProgramData\\APM\\policy.d` on Windows. Enterprise deployments often span Windows developer machines and CI runners. An enterprise reader provisioning policy hooks on Windows has no doc guidance.\n\nThe user hooks dir (`~/.apm/hooks/`) and the `APM_HOME` override are portable and correctly documented. Only the policy path is OS-specific and undocumented.",
2026-06-19T09:04:48.6617946Z       "suggested_fix": "Update the discovery table or add a callout:\n\n```\n| Priority     | Path (Linux/macOS)          | Path (Windows)                  | Who controls     |\n|--------------|-----------------------------|----------------------------------|------------------|\n| 1 (highest)  | `/etc/apm/policy.d/*.json`  | `C:\\ProgramData\\APM\\policy.d\\*.json` | Platform/IT team |\n| 2            | `~/.apm/hooks/*.json`       | `~/.apm/hooks/*.json`            | Individual user  |\n| 3            | `.apm/hooks.json`           | `.apm/hooks.json`                | Project          |\n```\n\nOr, more concisely, add a note beneath the existing table: 'On Windows, the policy path is `C:\\ProgramData\\APM\\policy.d`.'"
2026-06-19T09:04:48.6620224Z     },
2026-06-19T09:04:48.6620436Z     {
2026-06-19T09:04:48.6623359Z       "id": "doc-writer-4",
2026-06-19T09:04:48.6623798Z       "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-19T09:04:48.6630470Z       "category": "accuracy",
2026-06-19T09:04:48.6637555Z       "title": "Hook examples use bash-only field without cross-platform guidance",
2026-06-19T09:04:48.6644829Z       "file": "docs/src/content/docs/enterprise/lifecycle-hooks.md",
2026-06-19T09:04:48.6653189Z       "lines": "56-72",
2026-06-19T09:04:53.1931386Z       "description": "The hook format examples in both the opening JSON block (line 38) and the Command type section (line 57) use the `bash` field only:\n```json\n{ \"type\": \"command\", \"bash\": \"./scripts/notify.sh\", \"timeoutSec\": 10 }\n```\n`effective_command` is `return self.bash or self.command` — `bash` always wins when set. On Windows (where bash may not be on PATH), a hook with only the `bash` field will attempt to run a bash invocation and fail. The doc correctly notes (line 68-69) that `command` is 'cross-platform' and `bash` takes priority, but the examples never show the dual-field pattern that makes a hook portable.\n\nEnterprise platform teams deploying policy hooks across mixed OS fleets need to know to supply both fields.",
2026-06-19T09:04:55.3661476Z       "suggested_fix": "Add a sentence in the Command section after the priority note (line 74), and update at least one example to show the dual-field pattern:\n\n```markdown\nFor hooks deployed to mixed OS environments, supply both fields:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"type\": \"command\",\n  \"bash\": \"./scripts/notify.sh\",\n  \"command\": \"powershell.exe -File scripts/notify.ps1\",\n  \"timeoutSec\": 10\n}\n```\n\n`bash` runs on Linux/macOS; `command` runs on Windows where `bash` is absent.\n```"
2026-06-19T09:04:55.3663158Z     },

2026-06-19T09:13:34.8426322Z {
2026-06-19T09:13:34.8432021Z   "persona": "python-architect",
2026-06-19T09:13:34.8444023Z   "active": true,
2026-06-19T09:13:40.5058531Z   "summary": "Solid foundation: clean dataclass value objects, correct fire-and-forget exception isolation, good JSON schema versioning. Three architectural issues compound over time: (1) build-runner + build-event + fire logic is triplicated across InstallService, update.py, and uninstall/cli.py -- adding a fourth operation copies it again; (2) command hooks block the CLI synchronously for up to 30s each while HTTP hooks are async, a discrepancy undocumented in the PR; (3) effective_command always prefers bash regardless of platform, breaking Windows enterprise targets. Pre-install hooks fire before the ImportError guard, leaving analytics systems with an orphaned event on the defensive path. Full URL including query-string credentials is written verbatim to hooks.log. Private _hooks access from the CLI layer creates coupling that resists future refactoring.",
2026-06-19T09:13:40.5061569Z   "findings": [
2026-06-19T09:13:40.5061798Z     {
2026-06-19T09:13:40.5066986Z       "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-19T09:13:40.5075256Z       "summary": "Build-runner + build-event + fire logic triplicated across three files",
2026-06-19T09:13:51.6258815Z       "rationale": "InstallService._build_hook_runner/_build_event (service.py:113,129), _fire_update_hooks (update.py:615), and _fire_uninstall_hooks (uninstall/cli.py:313) each independently implement the same ~25-line pattern: resolve project_root from apm_package, call build_runner_from_context(), build a PackageInfo list, call LifecycleEvent.create(), call runner.fire(). The only variation is how the packages list is built (from get_apm_dependencies() vs a pre-built list). Adding a fourth operation type copies the pattern a fourth time.\n\nFix: add one free function to lifecycle_hooks.py:\n\n    def fire_event(\n        event_name: str,\n        *,\n        packages: list[PackageInfo],\n        scope: str,\n        project_root: str | None,\n        logger: Any = None,\n        verbose: bool = False,\n    ) -> None:\n        import contextlib\n        with contextlib.suppress(Exception):\n            runner = build_runner_from_context(logger=logger, verbose=verbose, project_root=project_root)\n            event = LifecycleEvent.create(event=event_name, packages=packages, scope=scope, working_directory=project_root)\n            runner.fire(event_name, event)\n\nAll three call sites reduce to: build the packages list, then call fire_event().\n\n**Design patterns**\n- Used in this PR: Dataclass-as-value-object (HookEntry, PackageInfo, LifecycleEvent -- appropriate and readable; consider adding frozen=True to LifecycleEvent since it crosses into the HTTP daemon thread); Strategy dispatch in execute_hook() (if/elif by hook_type, annotated as <<StrategyDispatch>> in the class diagram); Collect-then-render in discover_hooks() (accumulates from three sources before returning the full list).\n- Pragmatic suggestion: extract fire_event() free function to lifecycle_hooks.py -- collapses the triplication at exactly three call sites, the threshold where extraction pays off per the APM abstraction rule, without adding new abstraction layers.",
2026-06-19T09:13:51.6267892Z       "file": "src/apm_cli/core/lifecycle_hooks.py",

A hook file that supplies both bash (Unix script) and command (Windows-safe fallback) will always run bash -- which fails silently on Windows without WSL. The enterprise policy hook use case (policy.d/*.json deployed to AD-managed Windows machines) is a primary motivation for this PR. _get_policy_hooks_dir() already has Windows-specific logic (lifecycle_hooks.py:219), confirming Windows is an intended target, making this a concrete cross-platform gap.",

2026-06-19T09:13:58.2503211Z     {
2026-06-19T09:13:58.2509531Z       "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-19T09:13:58.2538487Z       "summary": "Command hooks block the CLI synchronously up to 30s each; HTTP hooks do not -- undocumented contract mismatch",
2026-06-19T09:14:01.6572056Z       "rationale": "_execute_command calls subprocess.run() at hook_executors.py:210, blocking the calling thread for up to 30s per hook (the default timeout). _execute_http fires a daemon thread (hook_executors.py:181) and returns immediately. With N command hooks on pre-install, the CLI blocks for N*30s before the install begins. The module docstring and PR body both describe all hooks as fire-and-forget, which is accurate only for HTTP. Enterprise pre-install use cases (analytics, CI gate callbacks) suggest command hooks should also be non-blocking unless the author explicitly intended serial pre-install gating.",
2026-06-19T09:14:01.6576447Z       "file": "src/apm_cli/core/hook_executors.py",
2026-06-19T09:14:01.6577601Z       "line": 210,
2026-06-19T09:14:03.8526437Z       "suggestion": "Either (a) run _execute_command in a daemon thread matching _execute_http, (b) reduce the default command timeout to 10s to match HTTP, or (c) add a blocking: bool field to HookEntry (default False) and document the sync vs async contract explicitly in the schema."
2026-06-19T09:14:03.8528180Z     },

If the import fails, pre-install has already fired but the install never executes and post-install never fires. Analytics or CI systems consuming hook events will observe a pre-install event with no matching post-install. The ImportError path is pragma: no cover -- defensive, so probability is very low, but the hook semantic contract is wrong regardless.",

2026-06-19T09:14:16.2251603Z       "rationale": "hooks.py:129: matching = [h for h in runner._hooks if h.event == event]. _hooks is private storage. If the runner is refactored to lazy-load hooks, shard by source, or filter on demand, this CLI-layer bypass breaks silently. The purpose -- count matching hooks before firing -- is a legitimate query the runner should expose as a public method.",
2026-06-19T09:14:16.2258015Z       "file": "src/apm_cli/commands/hooks.py",

+fire(event_name, event) None\n    }\n    class hook_executors:::touched {\n        <<StrategyDispatch>>\n        +execute_hook(hook, event) None\n        -_execute_http() daemon Thread\n 2026-06-19T09:21:57.3277736Z {
2026-06-19T09:21:57.3301519Z   "headline": "Do not merge: _expand_env_vars is a zero-exploit PAT exfiltration channel, security.md now carries two false claims, and correctness bugs silently kill the feature's display layer and test command — all five must be fixed in-branch before this PR is enterprise-safe.",
2026-06-19T09:22:02.0278224Z   "arbitration": "Supply-chain-security and auth-expert independently converge on the same root finding, making it the highest-weight signal in this panel: _expand_env_vars resolves any $VAR from os.environ with no denylist, and _build_hook_env passes dict(os.environ) to every command-hook subprocess. A project's .apm/hooks.json can specify headers: {Authorization: \"$GITHUB_APM_PAT\"} and ship the live PAT to any HTTPS endpoint on every apm install — no exploit required, no user error required. The auth-expert's evidence block (TestExpandEnvVars::test_does_not_expand_github_apm_pat, outcome: missing, principles: [secure-by-default, governed-by-policy]) confirms no test enforces this contract today; per arbitration policy, a missing test on a secure-by-default surface ranks at blocking regardless of the persona's original tier. Supply-chain's additional finding that C:\\ProgramData\\APM\\policy.d is world-writable on Windows is a real policy-tier trust failure; I distinguish it from the cross-platform exfiltration path in calibration but do not defer it — an ACL-check stub with a tracked follow-up is the minimum acceptable; shipping without acknowledgment is not.\n\nThree correctness bugs mean the feature will not work correctly out of the box. CLI-logging-expert identifies a permanent import failure: from apm_cli.utils.console import console references a name that does not exist in console.py — the import always fails silently, the Rich table and Panel are permanently dead, and every user sees only the bare click.echo fallback. DevX-UX-expert identifies that apm hooks test exits before its HTTP daemon threads complete, meaning the primary test workflow silently produces no output in hooks.log. Most critically for enterprise trust, supply-chain's finding that security.md lines 32–33 now carry false claims ('No arbitrary code execution', 'No network calls after install') must be corrected in this PR — these claims are relied upon by enterprise security reviewers and allowing them to persist after merge is a public contract violation. Doc-writer's corroborating finding that 'fire-and-forget' is factually wrong for command hooks (which block subprocess.run() for up to 30 seconds each) must be corrected in the module docstring and the documentation in the same commit.\n\nPython-architect and performance-expert agree on structural concerns — triplication of fire logic across three files, serial hook execution adding N×30s worst-case per phase, 30s default timeout 3–6× too generous for a synchronous CLI path — that compound over time but are suitable for tracked follow-up issues once the five blockers are resolved. The oss-growth-hacker's 'APM's npm-scripts moment' framing is exactly right: the three-tier discovery model and apm hooks init && apm hooks test flow are genuinely differentiated. That growth upside is worth protecting — which is precisely why the five blocking items must land before this PR ships. A token-exfiltration incident in week one would permanently damage the enterprise trust that the hooks feature is intended to deepen.",

The feature concept is strong: three-tier discovery is the right architecture, the init+test flow is genuine npm-parity ergonomics, and the growth upside is real. These are fixable issues measured in days, not weeks. Do not ship as-is to enterprise users who have staked their security posture on APM's documented promises."

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4261331Z

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4264066Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4266588Z   The Doc Writer is definitely active since the PR updates the lifecycle hooks documentation. The Performance Expert should be activated too—while there's no change to the core dependency download path, the lifecycle hooks inject synchronous subprocess calls into the install/update/uninstall workflows with a 30-second default timeout, which could impact performance.

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4494333Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4495096Z [189] ◐ Let me analyze this PR carefully from a supply-chain security perspective. I need to review the code snippets provided and produce a JSON response matching the panelist-return-schema.json format.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4495864Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4496343Z   Let me first load the relevant security documents and schema to ensure my review is grounded in the actual codebase contracts.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4496876Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4497150Z   Let me think through each security concern systematically:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4497482Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4497726Z   ## 1. shell=True + user-controlled cmd string
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4498026Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4498595Z   The `_execute_command` function uses `subprocess.run(cmd, shell=True, ...)` where `cmd` comes from `hook.effective_command` (either `hook.bash` or `hook.command`). 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4499231Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4499866Z   Key question: Who controls hook files? If hooks are defined in project's `.apm/hooks.json` committed to a public repo, then any machine that installs the project will execute those hooks. 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4500743Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4503133Z   This is essentially post-install code execution - one of the 8 threat model items APM must defend against. The `shell=True` with a user-controlled string creates a code injection surface if a malicious package could inject hooks into the project's `.apm/hooks.json` or if there's no explicit user consent before running hooks. However, if hooks are only in the project root `.apm/hooks.json` controlled by the project owner rather than packages, the threat model shifts to the project owner running commands on their own machine - which aligns with how npm handles lifecycle scripts during install, update, and uninstall operations.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4505561Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4507030Z   The `shell=True` parameter compounds the risk significantly: even if a hook command is supposed to invoke a specific binary, shell interpretation allows injection attacks if any part of the command string comes from package data like the package name, version, or path. This is a blocking severity issue since it represents the core post-install code execution threat.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4508406Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4509506Z   I'm also looking at environment variable expansion in HTTP headers - there's a function that replaces any environment variables found in header values, which could expose sensitive credentials if headers are ... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 4704 chars]
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4510456Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4510949Z [190] ✓ report_intent(intent: Reviewing PR supply-chain security)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4511225Z

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4515118Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4515631Z [194] ◐ Now I have a thorough understanding of the PR. Let me analyze each finding:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4516209Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4517835Z   1. **`[i]` in Rich Panel title (hooks.py:198)** — The `title="[i] Getting Started"` is using Rich markup notation. In Rich, `[i]` is an italic markup tag, not the literal string `[i]`. This means the Panel title will render as italic-formatted "Getting Started" text, not as `[i] Getting Started`. This is a real bug — the `[i]` symbol won't appear visually.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4519547Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4521005Z      The STATUS_SYMBOLS table shows `"info": "[i]"` — but since Rich interprets `[i]` as markup, passing it as a Panel title will silently strip `[i]` as an italic tag (or produce unexpected behavior). The fix is to escape the brackets: `title="\\[i] Getting Started"` or use `"[\\i] Getting Started"`.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4522292Z      
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4523445Z      To properly display the literal `[i]` symbol, I need to escape it with a backslash so Rich doesn't interpret it as a markup tag. The escaping approach `\\[i]` would render the intended `[i] Getting Started` text correctly.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4524696Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4526543Z      Now looking at the second issue with the HTTP hook — the `verbose_detail` message claims an event was "sent" immediately after the thread starts, but the actual completion happens asynchronously. This is misleading because the message fires before the thread finishes executing, so the event hasn't truly been sent yet when the log statement appears.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4528602Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4531073Z      There's also a stylistic inconsistency here: the `[i]` symbol is hardcoded directly into the message string rather than using the `STATUS_SYMBOLS` lookup like other parts of the code do. Since `verbose_detail` doesn't support a `symbol=` parameter, the code resorts to manually prefixing the symbol instead of using the standard approach. Still analyzing the implementation...

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4549640Z [202] ◐ Now I have a thorough picture. Let me check the init scaffold template closely and the `apm hooks` command behavior to understand the growth angle.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4550560Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4552738Z   Key observations:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4554033Z   1. The `apm hooks init` scaffold creates an echo command as the default - `echo 'Installed:' && cat` - this is an interesting choice but not very intuitive for a first-time user. A more compelling default would showcase the analytics angle.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4555646Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4557333Z   2. The doc is in `enterprise/lifecycle-hooks.md` - buried in the enterprise section. This is a significant growth concern. Most developers don't look at enterprise docs. The feature has broad applicability (Slack notifications, CI notifications, personal automation) but the doc placement signals it's enterprise-only.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4559385Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4559633Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4561813Z   3. The README mentions hooks in a yaml example comment but doesn't surface `apm hooks` as a CLI command, which hurts discoverability. The CLI itself does provide good inline guidance when you run `apm hooks` with nothing set up, but there's no CHANGELOG entry for this significant feature addition. The npm lifecycle hooks parallel is compelling but the docs don't explicitly draw that connection.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4564234Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4566011Z   4. The init scaffold outputs the JSON payload which helps understand the structure, but it's not a practical first example - something like a Slack notification or file logging would be more useful for getting started. The `apm hooks test` command is a nice DevX touch for dry-running without triggering real installs.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4567824Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4569475Z   5. There's no community hook registry or published examples, which is a missed opportunity since npm's scripts are commonly shared. The docs also don't establish the mental model connection to npm hooks, which would help JS developers quickly grasp the concept.

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4602225Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4602546Z [217] ◐ Key finding! In `_rich_echo`, line 130:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4602881Z   ```python
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4603211Z   console.print(message, style=style_str, highlight=False, markup=False)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4603723Z   ```
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4604004Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4605093Z   Note `markup=False`! This means Rich markup is DISABLED in `_rich_echo`. So `[i]` in the message string is safe — it will render as the literal string `[i]`.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4605823Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4606303Z   But in `hooks.py:191-200`, the Panel is constructed directly:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4606814Z   ```python
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4607434Z   console.print(
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4607799Z       Panel(
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4608223Z           "[bold]Next steps:[/bold]\n\n"
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4608823Z           f"  1. Edit [cyan]{target_file.relative_to(Path.cwd())}[/cyan] "
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4609326Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4609687Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4610013Z   ```
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4610384Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4610759Z   Building the Panel with instructions...
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4611220Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4612514Z   The key difference is that this `console.print(Panel(...))` call bypasses `_rich_echo` entirely, so the `markup=False` protection doesn't apply. Rich's default behavior is to parse markup, which means `[i]` in the Panel title will actually be interpreted as italic formatting rather than rendered as literal text.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4613690Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4614762Z   I need to trace where this `console` variable comes from in hooks.py to understand what instance is being used and whether it has any custom configuration that might affect markup handling.

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4711248Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4711665Z [254] ◐ Now I have all the information I need to generate the documentation review. Let me analyze the key findings:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4712202Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4712865Z   1. **"fire-and-forget" language accuracy**: The doc says on line 10: "Hooks are fire-and-forget: a failing hook never blocks the CLI." But this is actually **only partially true**:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4714489Z      - **HTTP hooks**: Truly fire-and-forget — they run in a daemon thread (`threading.Thread(target=_send, daemon=True)`) and do NOT block the CLI. ✓
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4715757Z      - **Command hooks**: **Synchronous** via `subprocess.run()` — they DO block the CLI for up to 30 seconds (or whatever `timeoutSec` is set to). 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4716456Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4718014Z   This inaccuracy appears in multiple places: the module docstring in lifecycle_hooks.py claims "All hooks are fire-and-forget," and the security considerations section repeats this claim with configurable timeouts. But command hooks are synchronous and definitely block execution, so these statements are misleading for that hook type.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4719326Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4722292Z   2. **Windows/cross-platform `command` vs `bash` distinction**: The doc needs to clarify the platform-specific behavior around command hooks. not explicit enough for Windows users. Policy hooks can't be disabled according to the docs, and that checks out with the code. The `commands.md` file is definitely missing an `apm hooks` section — that's a real gap. The security section has some issues too: it mentions "fire-and-forget" for commands which doesn't match the actual behavior, and while it's correct that verbose mode logs hook failures, the claim that they never block the CLI only applies to HTTP hooks, not local ones. Let me look at the sidebar ordering next.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4724640Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4726303Z   I'm checking the enterprise docs structure to see how this policy hooks doc should be ordered alongside the other governance pages. 2026-06-19T09:27:15.4746464Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4746742Z [268] ◐ Now I have a complete picture. Let me analyze all the findings:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4747291Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4747921Z   1. **`requests` IS a declared dependency** - confirmed in pyproject.toml and uv.lock. So no finding there.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4748567Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4749749Z   2. **`shell=True` with user-controlled command string** - This is the post-install code execution threat. The command comes from `hook.effective_command` which is `hook.bash or hook.command` - both from the hook JSON file. The hook JSON files come from:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4750780Z      - `/etc/apm/policy.d/*.json` (admin-owned)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4751149Z      - `~/.apm/hooks/*.json` (user-owned)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4751494Z      - `.apm/hooks.json` (project-level)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4751944Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4753878Z   The project-level hooks file is where the real concern lies. If someone commits `.apm/hooks.json` to a public repository and another developer installs that repo as a dependency, the hooks would execute during their installation. However, looking at how `discover_hooks()` works, it loads from the current project's root directory using `Path.cwd()`, not from the installed package's directory, so hooks only run from the installing project itself, not from dependencies.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4755989Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4758578Z   But I need to verify this by tracing through the actual install command to see whether `project_root` gets set to the installed package's location or stays as the current working directory. Also, there's a significant security issue with `_expand_env_vars` - a hook author can inject environment variables like `$GITHUB_TOKEN` into headers and exfiltrate them to external URLs. For project-level hooks, this means the project owner could do this, which is less of a supply chain risk but still a social engineering concern if developers blindly copy committed hook files. For user-level hooks, it's the user's own tokens at risk, and for policy hooks there are different implications.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4761307Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4762771Z   The real danger emerges if this framework gets used for project hooks that are committed to repositories and shared - it becomes a standard attack vector where developers unknowingly run hooks that steal their authentication tokens. There's also the issue of credentials persisting in the... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 6025 chars]
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4764061Z

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4770309Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4770881Z [274] ◐ Now I have a thorough understanding of the PR. Let me analyze the CLI UX from a developer experience perspective and formulate my findings.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4771530Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4772150Z   Key UX issues I've identified:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4772653Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4773090Z   ## Required (blocking)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4773604Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4776174Z   1. **Windows scaffold template uses `bash` field** - The `_INIT_TEMPLATE` in hooks.py line 33 generates a starter with `"bash": "echo 'Installed:' && cat"`. This is Unix-only. On Windows, this command will fail silently (since hook failures are swallowed). A Windows user following `apm hooks init` → `apm hooks test` → gets no error feedback, just a mysteriously empty log. The template should include both `bash` and `command` fields, or at minimum call out the Windows incompatibility.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4777938Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4778226Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4780504Z   2. **`apm hooks test` success message is misleading for HTTP hooks** - The success message `"'{event}' event fired. Check ~/.apm/logs/hooks.log for output."` prints after `runner.fire()` returns, but HTTP hooks run asynchronously in daemon threads. For HTTP hooks, this message appears before the request completes or fails, so the log may still be empty. This creates a false sense of completion when the user is actually trying to debug webhook connectivity.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4782708Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4783060Z   ## Recommended
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4783465Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4786165Z   3. **30-second synchronous blocking is invisible to users** - Command hooks run via `subprocess.run()` with a default 30-second timeout, and during `apm install`, the CLI silently blocks for up to 30 seconds with no progress indicator when a command hook is registered. This breaks the principle of keeping operations fast and quiet on the happy path—a user seeing the command hang will likely Ctrl-C and report a bug. The pre/post-install hooks in `install/service.py` run synchronously before and after the pipeline with no feedback to the user.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4788141Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4789505Z   4. **Private attribute access in `apm hooks test`** - The code directly accesses `runner._hooks` to pre-filter hooks, which is fragile if the field gets renamed or wrapped as a property. This also creates a divergence where `apm hooks test` uses the private attribute while `runner.fir... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 7806 chars]
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4790888Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4792219Z [275] ✓ $ cat /tmp/1781859750128-copilot-tool-output-zb4x9t.txt | python3 -c " import sys, json data = json.load(sys.stdin) text = data[0]['content'][0]['text'] # Find the update.py diff idx = text.find('diff --git a/src/apm_cli/commands/update.py') end = text.find('diff --git a/src/apm_cli', idx+1) print(tex...
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4793164Z

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4795429Z

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4799622Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4800741Z [280] ✓ $ grep -n "security\|arbitrary code\|No.*execution\|post.install\|hook" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/docs/src/content/docs/enterprise/security.md | head -30
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4801761Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4802556Z [281] ◐ Now I have a thorough picture of the implementation. Let me analyze the performance characteristics carefully:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4803400Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4804532Z   1. **Discovery overhead** - `discover_hooks()` runs on every `install`, `update`, and `uninstall` call. It performs:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4805534Z      - `platform.system()` call (cheap, in-process)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4806309Z      - `_get_policy_hooks_dir().is_dir()` - one syscall to check `/etc/apm/policy.d`
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4807187Z      - `_get_user_hooks_dir().is_dir()` - one syscall to check `~/.apm/hooks/`
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4808077Z      - `_get_project_hooks_file().is_file()` - one syscall to check `.apm/hooks.json`
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4808771Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4810217Z      **No caching** means `build_runner_from_context` -> `discover_hooks` gets called repeatedly: once per install, twice per update (pre and post hooks each trigger a separate discovery), and the pattern continues through uninstall operations.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4811739Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4813320Z   2. **Synchronous command execution** - `subprocess.run()` with `shell=True` and a 30-second default timeout blocks everything. The hook runner iterates through matching hooks serially, executing each one to completion before moving to the next.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4815119Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4816915Z   3. **Worst-case latency** - With 7 command hooks per install phase (3 policy + 3 user + 1 project), running serially at 30 seconds each, that's 210 seconds just for pre-install, and the same for post-install.  Total worst case is 420 seconds or 7 minutes added to a single install operation. The same math applies to updates and uninstalls.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4818914Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4820343Z   4. **Double discovery overhead** - For update and uninstall operations, the hook discovery runs separately for pre and post events, doubling the filesystem syscalls from 3 to 6. On slower storage like NFS, this compounds the latency problem significantly.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4821786Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4823080Z   5. **No optimization for the no-hooks case** - Every operation pays the discovery cost even when no hooks are configured, though the code does return early if directories don't exist. Still, we're calling `is_dir()` on all three paths regardless.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4824656Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4825711Z   6. **Daemon threads for HTTP hooks** - The HTTP hook implementation uses fire-and-forget daemon threads without waiting for completion, whi... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 10605 chars]
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4826964Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4827939Z [282] ✓ $ find /home/runner/work/apm/apm -name "panelist-return-schema.json" 2>/dev/null; find /home/runner/work/apm/apm -name "*.schema.json" | head -10
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4828766Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4830051Z [283] ✓ $ # Check the LifecycleHookRunner.fire() loop more carefully for concurrency grep -n "for hook\|ThreadPool\|concurrent\|asyncio\|thread" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/core/lifecycle_hooks.py
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4831234Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4832320Z [284] ◐ I see at lines 170-179 in hook_executors.py: the `_append_to_hook_log` call passes `url` directly as the `target` parameter. Looking at the `_send()` closure:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4833427Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4833786Z   ```python
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4834310Z   def _send() -> None:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4834747Z       try:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4835121Z           import requests
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4835590Z           resp = requests.post(url, ...)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4836110Z           _append_to_hook_log(
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4836570Z               event_name,
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4836989Z               "http",
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4837536Z               url,  # <-- the full URL is logged here, including any userinfo
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4838211Z               stdout=f"HTTP {resp.status_code}",
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4838782Z               status="ok" if resp.ok else "error",
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4839298Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4839673Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4840010Z   ```
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4840338Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4840696Z   Still writing error handling...
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4841155Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4843026Z   The `url` gets passed directly to the hook log without any sanitization, so if it contains embedded credentials like `******host.com/webhook`, those would end up in the log file. That's a security issue. There's an HTTPS-only check in place, but that doesn't address the credential exposure problem.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4844953Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4846381Z   Looking at the test suite, I don't see any tests covering credential sanitization in URLs—just tests for rejecting HTTP URLs, handling missing URLs, daemon thread dispatch, and verbose hostname logging.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4847576Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4849400Z   Now checking the pre-install hook ordering issue in service.py, it looks like the hook runner fires the pre-install event before the ImportError guard for the apm_cli import, which could be problematic. installation never completes. So the pre-install hook fires, then if the import fails, we skip the actual installation and never fire post-install. That's the ordering bug—the lifecycle hooks are out of sync with whether the installation actually succeeds.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4850954Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4852647Z   Now I'm checking if there's a test that verifies this ordering, specifically whether pre-install fires before the pipeline runs. Looking through the test file, I see the tests mock out `run_install_pipeline` but they don't seem to assert on the hook runner's behavior or the sequence of events. So the hook runner actually executes in the test environment, but since the fake APM package returns an empty dependency list, no hooks get triggered anyway. The test only validates that the ... 2026-06-19T09:27:15.4858012Z [289] ◐ Now I have a critical finding. The `security.md` at line 33 says:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4858696Z   > **No arbitrary code execution.** APM does not execute scripts from packages, evaluate expressions in templates, or run downloaded code.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4859272Z

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4861797Z

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4864596Z   This is in a threat table listing "No code execution" as the mitigation against post-install scripts. 2026-06-19T09:27:15.4911506Z [300] ◐ Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me also check what the `effective_command` property does on Windows — on Windows, `bash` is still returned as `bash or command`, and then it's run with `shell=True`. On Windows, `shell=True` uses `cmd.exe`, so `echo 'Installed:' && cat` would:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4912715Z   1. `echo 'Installed:'` — prints `'Installed:'` (with literal quotes, but close enough)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4913215Z   2. `cat` — not a standard Windows command, fails
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4913554Z   3. But this failure is swallowed silently
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4913987Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4915458Z   This confirms the Windows scaffold issue. The generated `_INIT_TEMPLATE` with `"bash": "echo 'Installed:' && cat"` will run through `subprocess.run(cmd, shell=True)` on Windows via `cmd.exe`, fail at `cat`, and the error will be silently swallowed (logged to hooks.log only). The user sees `"'{event}' event fired. Check ~/.apm/logs/hooks.log for output."` and has no idea the hook failed.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4916705Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4918480Z   There's an even more critical issue with `apm hooks test` and daemon threads. HTTP hooks execute in daemon threads, and when `fire()` calls `_execute_http()`, it spawns a daemon thread to send the request. But daemon threads get terminated when the main thread exits — so if `apm hooks test` completes and exits immediately after printing success, the daemon thread may never finish, meaning the log entry the user is told to check might never actually be written.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4919977Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4921049Z   This isn't just misleading output; it's a functional correctness problem. The test command is supposed to verify the hook wiring works, but for HTTP hooks it can't because the daemon thread gets killed before the request completes. The command returns success, tells the user to check logs, but those logs may never exist.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4922154Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4923782Z   So my findings break down into two categories: the Windows scaffold issue and the broken HTTP test command are blocking problems that make core functionality unusable, while the other issues—the blocking install command, the real HTTP requests in a dry-run, the undiscoverable list command, the markup rendering bug, and validation errors—are recommended f... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2663 chars]

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4929213Z [302] ◐ So `console` is NOT a module-level export from `console.py`. The `from apm_cli.utils.console import console` in hooks.py will raise an `ImportError` (or `AttributeError` which would be caught by `NameError`? Actually it would be `ImportError`). Since both usages are wrapped in `except (ImportError, NameError):`, this means the Rich Panel/Table rendering path will never execute in practice! The code will always fall through to the plaintext `click.echo` fallback.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4930788Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4931261Z   Wait, but this could be a legitimate bug introduced in this PR. The `console` doesn't exist at module level in console.py, so:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4931784Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4931969Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4936670Z   Both `hooks()` and `hooks_init()` attempt to import `console` from the utils module, but since it's not defined there, the import fails silently and falls back to the plaintext output. This is definitely a bug—the Rich formatting never actually gets used. The Panel title markup issue is actually moot since the import failure prevents it from rendering at all, but if that were fixed, Rich would interpret `[i]` as italic formatting and strip the bracket symbol from the display. The core problem remains that this entire feature is broken at the UX layer — users never see the table or onboarding panel because the console object can't be imported. The message wording is misleading since the HTTP request is async and hasn't actually been sent yet—it should say "dispatching" or "queued" instead. Looking at the `[i]` prefixes scattered throughout the hook executor and lifecycle files, they're actually working as intended since `_rich_echo` is called with `markup=False`, so the literal `[i]` renders correctly and matches the STATUS_SYMBOLS pattern. But there's a catch: `verbose_detail` doesn't have a `symbol=` parameter, so the code is manually prepending `[i]` as a string.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4940644Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4941727Z   This creates a maintenance issue—if someone changes STATUS_SYMBOLS["info"] later, these hardcoded prefixes would fall out of sync. It's more of a stylistic problem than a functional bug. The Panel title with `[i]` mark... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 7088 chars]

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4961234Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4961599Z [313] ◐ Now I have all the information I need to construct the JSON response. Let me compile the findings:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4962056Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4962477Z   1. **Serial command hook execution in fire() loop** - recommended
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4962975Z      - `lifecycle_hooks.py:306` - `for hook in matching:` - sequential, no concurrency
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4963534Z      - 7 hooks (3 policy + 3 user + 1 project) * 2 phases * 30s = 420s worst-case per install
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4963955Z      - Even at 2s/hook: 28s added
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4964333Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4964597Z   2. **Double discovery in update/uninstall** - recommended
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4965200Z      - `update.py:531,606` and `uninstall/cli.py:87,298` call `build_runner_from_context` (-> `discover_hooks`) twice per operation
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4965914Z      - `service.py` correctly builds the runner once and fires it twice, avoiding redundant stat syscalls
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4966372Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4966613Z   3. **30s default command hook timeout** - recommended
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4967071Z      - `hook_executors.py:35` sets a very long synchronous timeout that blocks the CLI path
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4967488Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4967764Z   4. **No process-level cache for zero-hooks deployments** - nit
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4968373Z      - `discover_hooks()` lacks caching and runs on every CLI invocation, adding unnecessary overhead when no hooks are present
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4968896Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4969217Z   5. **Daemon threads for HTTP hooks lack graceful shutdown** - correctness issue
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4970031Z      - Fire-and-forget execution on daemon threads avoids blocking wall-time, but no join on exit means events can be silently lost during fast shutdowns, affecting audit trails
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4970704Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4970938Z   6. **Shell subprocess spawning overhead** - minor
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4971683Z      - Using `shell=True` adds a small but measurable cost from spawning `/bin/sh -c` on each invocation (5-20ms on Linux, ~50ms on Windows), which compounds across multiple command hooks
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4972364Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4973297Z   Now I'm structuring the key findings: serial command hook execution creates unbounded delays in the install hot path with a worst-case of 420 seconds across 7 hooks and 2 phases, and there's inconsistent hook discovery logic between install versus update and uninstall commands.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4974671Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4975779Z   The 30-second default timeout for command hooks is excessive for a synchronous CLI operation—it should be 5-10 seconds instead. There's also redundant filesystem stat calls on every operation when no hooks exist, and HTTP ... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2135 chars]
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4976667Z

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4990320Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4991675Z   Looking at the blocking issues now: the document claims hooks are "fire-and-forget" in the opening and security sections, but that's inaccurate. Command hooks use synchronous subprocess calls that block for up to 30 seconds, while HTTP hooks use daemon threads for async execution — so the behavior varies significantly depending on hook type.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4992837Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4993600Z   There's also a gap in the maintained CLI reference skill; `commands.md` covers every other command group but has no section for `apm hooks`, which creates a disconnect between the documentation site and what agents can access.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4994649Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4996051Z   On the recommended side, the Windows policy path (`C:\ProgramData\APM\policy.d`) isn't documented anywhere — only the Unix path appears in the discovery table, which is a problem for enterprise Windows deployments. The page also lacks cross-references to related governance and security documentation that would help users understand the broader context.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4997421Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4998268Z   The security section has another issue where it doesn't clearly separate APM's built-in protection layer from the `apm audit` tool, and it incorrectly describes all hooks as "fire-and-forget" in multiple pla... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 4360 chars]

2026-06-19T09:27:15.5015004Z [322] ◐ Now I have all the information I need. Let me compile the final JSON response.

2026-06-19T09:27:15.5015628Z   Key facts established:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5016092Z   1. `LifecycleHookRunner.fire()` at line 306 is a serial `for hook in matching:` loop with no concurrency.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5016763Z   2. `_execute_command()` at line 210 calls `subprocess.run(..., shell=True, timeout=30)` synchronously.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5017709Z   3. `_fire_update_hooks()` at line 642 calls `build_runner_from_context()` (which calls `discover_hooks()`) - this is called TWICE per update: once for pre-update (line 531) and once for post-update (line 606).
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5018454Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5018641Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5019195Z   4. The same redundant discovery pattern appears in `_fire_uninstall_hooks()`, while `service.py` does it right by building the runner once and reusing it.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5020326Z   5. There's no caching on `discover_hooks()`, each hook command has a 30-second timeout, and HTTP hooks spawn daemon threads without waiting for them to complete.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5021169Z   6. In the worst case with 7 command hooks per event, that's 210 seconds per phase, 420 seconds total per operation.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5021819Z   7. Even with faster 2-second hooks, we're still adding 28 seconds to the install wall-time.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5022239Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5022440Z   Now I'm writing the JSON output.

2026-06-19T09:27:15.5046022Z [332] ◐ Now I have a clear picture. The `_get_console()` function exists in two places:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5046605Z   1. `apm_cli.utils.console._get_console()` — the actual singleton factory
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5047175Z   2. `apm_cli.commands._helpers._get_console()` — a thin wrapper around the same
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5047568Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5047786Z   The correct pattern for commands is:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5048066Z   ```python
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5048290Z   from ._helpers import _get_console
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5048552Z   # or
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5048800Z   from apm_cli.utils.console import _get_console
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5049111Z   # then inside the function:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5049379Z   console = _get_console()
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5049620Z   ```
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5049812Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5050700Z   `hooks.py` does `from apm_cli.utils.console import console` (trying to import a non-existent module-level `console` variable). This always raises `ImportError`, and the `except (ImportError, NameError)` block catches it, falling back to click.echo text output.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5051628Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5051816Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5054732Z   The fix is to import `_get_console` instead and call it to get the console instance, then handle the Panel title markup issue by either disabling markup or escaping the bracket character. Now I'm compiling the findings for the JSON output. There's also a misleading message at hook_executors.py:185 that says the event was "sent" when it's actually being dispatched asynchronously in a background thread — should say "dispatching" instead. Additionally, several calls to `verbose_detail()` are hardcoding the `[i]` symbol directly rather than using `STATUS_SYMBOLS["info"]` like the rest of the codebase does, and the function doesn't currently support a `symbol=` parameter to make this consistent.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5056996Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5058391Z   The hooks module is also bypassing the CommandLogger lifecycle entirely by calling `_rich_*` helpers directly, which violates the APM output architecture that requires CommandLogger as the foundation for all commands. Finally, the success message for `apm hooks test` claims to check the logs, but for HTTP hooks that dispatch asynchronously, the log entry might not exist yet, making the success confirmation premature.

2026-06-19T09:27:15.5097945Z     "findings": [
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5098163Z       {
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5098374Z         "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5099040Z         "summary": "Serial command hook execution multiplies timeout worst-case into install/update/uninstall wall-time",
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5099608Z         "file": "src/apm_cli/core/lifecycle_hooks.py",
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5099931Z         "line": 306,
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5104197Z         "rationale": "LifecycleHookRunner.fire() iterates `for hook in matching:` and calls execute_hook() synchronously for each entry. execute_hook() dispatches to _execute_command() which calls subprocess.run(..., shell=True, timeout=30) -- a blocking call. With 3 policy + 3 user + 1 project command hooks (7 total), fire() blocks for sum(min(hook_ms, 30000ms)) per event phase. Worst case: 7 hooks * 30s * 2 phases (pre+post) = 420s = 7 minutes of serial subprocess wait added to a single install. Even a fast-hook scenario (each hook completes in 2s): 7 * 2s * 2 phases = 28s of added wall-time. The install core pipeline (resolve + fetch) typically runs in 5-60s; 28s of serial hook overhead doubles that budget for users with a typical hook suite. HTTP hooks correctly fire in daemon threads (hook_executors.py:181) and add zero wall-time. Command hooks must receive the same treatment: each should dispatch to a thread so they execute concurrently within a phase. The fire() loop can then join the threads before returning if completion semantics are needed, or stay truly fire-and-forget to match the documented contract. subprocess.run with shell=True also spawns an extra shell process (~10-20ms on Linux, ~50ms on Windows) per hook invocation; for 7 hooks that adds ~70-350ms of deterministic process-spawn overhead beyond hook execution time.",

2026-06-19T09:27:15.5129326Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5130804Z   This is a deliberate design pattern—pre-install fires, then the pipeline runs, then post-install fires—but it creates an edge case where the pre-install hook completes successfully even if the pipeline import fails. The test coverage for this is incomplete because the tests mock the pipeline but don't verify that the hook runner is being called or assert on its behavior.

Now I'm examining the credential leakage risk more carefully. 2026-06-19T09:27:15.5147747Z [345] ◐ Now I have enough information to complete my analysis. Let me summarize my findings:1. **InstallService pre/post-install hooks are not tested at the service boundary**: The `test_service.py` tests verify pipeline delegation but do NOT assert that `runner.fire("pre-install", ...)` and `runner.fire("post-install", ...)` are called. The hook runner is built but any hook firing is transparent to the test. Since `fake_apm_package.get_apm_dependencies()` returns an empty list (MagicMock default), no hooks are discovered or fired. There's no test that provides hooks and asserts they're fired through the service.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5149728Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5149926Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5150962Z   2. **The update and uninstall hook functions are completely untested**: Searching the test tree shows no references to `_fire_update_hooks` or `_fire_uninstall_hooks`, even though these functions are invoked in the actual code paths for updates and uninstalls. These hook firing mechanisms have zero test coverage.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5152047Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5153217Z   3. **Environment variable expansion in HTTP headers lacks integration testing**: While `_expand_env_vars` is tested in isolation, there's no test verifying that when a hook with headers containing variables like `$TOKEN` actually executes via `_execute_http`, the expanded values get passed through correctly. If the wiring breaks, nothing would catch it.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5154539Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5155434Z   4. **Hook log URLs aren't tested for credential sanitization**: The `_append_to_hook_log` function writes the full URL to the log file, including any embedded credentials in the `user:pass@host` format. There's no test ensuring these credentials are stripped before logging.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5156363Z

2026-06-19T09:27:15.5162849Z [349] ◐ Now I have a thorough understanding of the PR. Let me compile my findings as the python-architect panelist.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5163336Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5163552Z   ### Key Architectural Findings:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5163826Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5164255Z   1. **BLOCKING: Pre-install hooks fire before ImportError guard** (service.py:70)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5164982Z      - `runner.fire("pre-install", event)` runs at line 70, BEFORE the `try: from apm_cli.install.pipeline import run_install_pipeline` at line 75.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5166150Z      - If the ImportError guard fires (line 76), the pre-install hook has already run but the install never happens. This means the `pre-install` hook fires but `post-install` never fires, leaving the system in an inconsistent state.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5167855Z      - This is a correctness issue where hooks can fire without matching actual operations, which could confuse analytics systems in enterprises relying on hook events. While the ImportError is marked as defensive and unlikely, it's still a real path that breaks the hook contract. I'm marking this as recommended severity.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5168953Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5169386Z   2. **RECOMMENDED: Triplication of build-runner + build-event logic**
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5169756Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5171684Z      - I'm noticing the same pattern repeated across three places: `_fire_update_hooks`, `_fire_uninstall_hooks`, and `InstallService`'s hook-building methods all follow the same sequence of getting the project root, constructing a runner, building package info, creating a lifecycle event, and firing it. The uninstall version has a minor variation where it accepts a packages list directly instead of calling `get_apm_dependencies()`. The fix is to consolidate this into a single `fire_event()` function in `lifecycle_hooks.py` that handles all the runner and event construction, eliminating the duplication.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5173627Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5175200Z   Now I'm looking at another issue: HTTP hooks run asynchronously in a daemon thread, but command hooks block the CLI with `subprocess.run()` and a 30-second timeout. This creates an inconsistency where multiple command hooks could block for up to 90 seconds total. The documentation mentions "fire-and-forget" behavior but doesn't clarify the blocking nature or timeout constraints. The recommenda... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 35258 chars]

For creating new items, use create_issue, create_discussion, or create_pull_request instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 2 comment(s) can be added. If omitted, the tool will attempt to resolve the target from the current workflow context (triggering issue, PR, or discussion). If auto-targeting fails (e.g., for schedule or workflow_dispatch triggers), the tool call will fail with an error.","type":["number","string"]},"pr":{"description":"Alias for item_number when targeting a pull request. Only applicable for discussion comments — ignored for issue and pull request comments.","type":"string"},"secrecy":{"description":"Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., \"public\", \"internal\", \"private\").","type":"string"},"temporary_id":{"description":"Unique temporary identifier for this comment. 2026-06-19T09:27:15.7539290Z {"timestamp":"2026-06-19T08:57:18.131Z","event":"rpc_response","_schema":"rpc-message/v2","direction":"IN","server_id":"github","payload":{"id":1,"jsonrpc":"2.0","result":{"content":[{"text":"{\"number\":1798,\"title\":\"feat: add general-purpose lifecycle hooks framework (#1529)\",\"body\":\"## TL;DR\\n\\nAdds a Copilot CLI-aligned lifecycle hooks framework that fires events on install, update, and uninstall -- enabling enterprise analytics, CI integrations, and custom automation via standalone JSON hook files.\\n\\n## Problem (WHY)\\n\\nEnterprise users need opt-in analytics and extensibility around package lifecycle events (install, update, uninstall). There was no event system to hook into -- users could not build integrations like Slack notifications, audit logging, or telemetry collection on top of APM\\u0026#39;s package operations. See #1529.\\n\\n## Approach (WHAT)\\n\\nFollow the Copilot CLI hooks model: standalone JSON hook files discovered from well-known locations, with two hook types (command and HTTP). All hooks are additive across sources and policy hooks cannot be disabled.\\n\\n### Discovery sources (priority order)\\n\\n| Priority | Source | Path | Who controls |\\n|----------|--------|------|--------------|\\n| 1 | Policy | `/etc/apm/policy.d/*.json` | Platform/IT team |\\n| 2 | User | `~/.apm/hooks/*.json` | Individual user |\\n| 3 | Project | `.apm/hooks.json` | Project |\\n\\n### Lifecycle events\\n\\n`pre-install`, `post-install`, `pre-update`, `post-update`, `pre-uninstall`, `post-uninstall`\\n\\n### Hook types\\n\\n- **command** -- runs a shell command, receives event JSON on stdin\\n- **http** -- sends HTTPS POST with event JSON body, supports header env-var expansion\\n\\n### CLI commands\\n\\n- `apm hooks` -- list all discovered hooks\\n- `apm hooks init` -- scaffold a starter `.apm/hooks.json`\\n- `apm hooks test` -- dry-run a synthetic event\\n- `apm hooks validate` -- check all hook files for errors\\n\\n### Hook output logging\\n\\nAll hook execution output is logged to `~/.apm/logs/hooks.log` with timestamps, event name, hook type, target, status, and stdout/stderr.\\n\\n## Implementation (HOW)\\n\\n### New files\\n- `src/apm_cli/core/lifecycle_hooks.py` -- HookEntry model, discovery, runner\\n- `src/apm_cli/core/hook_executors.py` -- command/HTTP executors, log file writer\\n- `src/apm_cli/commands/hooks.py` -- CLI group with init/test/validate subcommands\\n- `docs/src/content/docs/enterprise/lifecycle-hooks.md` -- full documentation\\n\\n### Modified files\\n- `src/apm_cli/install/service.py` -- fire pre/post-install hooks\\n- `src/apm_cli/commands/uninstall/cli.py` -- fire pre/post-uninstall hooks\\n- `src/apm_cli/commands/update.py` -- fire pre/post-update hooks\\n- `src/apm_cli/cli.py` -- register hooks command group\\n\\n### Tests\\n- `tests/unit/core/test_lifecycle_hooks.py` -- 33 tests (schema, parsing, discovery, runner)\\n- `tests/unit/core/test_hook_executors.py` -- 35 tests (executors, env expansion, cwd, logging)\\n- `tests/unit/commands/test_hooks.py` -- 25 tests (CLI commands)\\n\\n## Validation\\n\\n- All 17,232 unit tests pass\\n- Full lint chain passes (ruff check, ruff format, pylint R0801, auth-signal lint)\\n- Integration tested all 6 lifecycle events end-to-end\\n- Hook output log file verified with real command hooks\\n\\nCloses
